summary: Dutch architect and furniture designer, a central figure in the De Stijl movement. Best known for the Rietveld Schroder House in Utrecht (featuring sliding partitions and a 'disappearing corner') and the Red and Blue Chair. Also designed furniture such as the Zig-Zag Chair and contributed to the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam.

works.name: Rietveld Schroder House (1924)
works.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site in Utrecht, designed for Truus Schroder. Exemplifies De Stijl architecture with its open floor plan, sliding partitions, and primary color scheme. Wikipedia

The sliding walls and window coloring of the Eames's Case Study House No. 8 displays the influence of this movement. The best-known architectural work of this movement is Gerrit Rietveld's Schroder House.

works.name: Red and Blue Chair (1917/1923)
works.description: Iconic piece of De Stijl furniture. Originally unstained wood, Rietveld later painted it in primary colors (red, blue, yellow, black) after joining De Stijl. Uses a distinctive joint of three orthogonal battens. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Gerrit Rietveld (1888-1964) was a Dutch architect and furniture designer who became one of the principal figures of the De Stijl movement. In quiz bowl, he appears most frequently in connection with his two most iconic works: the Rietveld Schroder House and the Red and Blue Chair.

The Rietveld Schroder House (1924) in Utrecht is the most heavily clued work. Key clues include its 'disappearing corner' (created by opening the dining room windows), its upper floor with no stationary walls that can be divided using sliding and revolving panels, and its construction for Truus Schroder, who received co-designer credit. A harder clue mentions the black spiral staircase connecting to a private room off-limits to visitors. The house is also linked to the Eames Case Study House No. 8, which incorporated De Stijl principles.

The Red and Blue Chair is the second most important work. It was originally made of unstained wood, and Bart van der Leck suggested adding color. Rietveld then painted it in primary colors after joining De Stijl. The chair uses a distinctive joint of three orthogonal battens (sometimes called a 'Rietveld joint') and was inscribed with the poem Der Aesthet by Christian Morgenstern.

Other works that appear include the Zig-Zag Chair (a Z-shaped design), the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam (where Rietveld designed one wing with a spiral staircase), and the 1955 Sonsbeek Pavilion (which revived interest in De Stijl). Rietveld is almost always clued as a Dutch architect associated with De Stijl, and questions about him frequently appear alongside Piet Mondrian, Theo van Doesburg, and the broader De Stijl movement.
